patterns.note: Non-mutating "derive a new value" accessor — receiver is untouched.

methods.rationale: Returns a brand-new NSString that the caller owns; the argument is only read during concatenation — it's neither copied nor retained, so it's "borrowed" for the duration of the call.

patterns.note: Inserts/replaces an entry — the receiver's contents change in place.

methods.rationale: Classic dictionary insertion: the value (arg 0) is retained, but the key (arg 1) is *copied* — that's why mutable keys can't "come back to bite you" -> the dictionary holds an immutable snapshot of the key.

patterns.note: Performs disk I/O; "atomically:" routes through a temp file then renames into place.

methods.rationale: Returns a BOOL success flag and writes via the trailing NSError** — the canonical Cocoa "return NO and populate *error" idiom; the URL and error pointer are merely borrowed, nothing is retained past the call.

patterns.note: Mutates the receiver in place — only valid on NSMutableString.

methods.rationale: An in-place edit: it splices the replacement into the receiver's backing store and returns nothing — the replacement string is read ("borrowed") to copy its characters, never retained.

patterns.note: Pure functional sort — produces a new array, receiver is left intact.

methods.rationale: The comparator is invoked synchronously during the call to order the elements — it does not "escape" past the method's return — and a fresh, caller-owned sorted array is handed back.

patterns.note: Block is retained and replayed on every matching notification until removeObserver: is called.
Vends an opaque observer token the caller must hold onto.

methods.rationale: The block is *stored* — not merely escaping — and re-invoked on the supplied queue for each matching notification; the returned token is owned by the caller, who is responsible for its lifetime: "you must call removeObserver: or you'll leak the registration."

patterns.note: Swift "async throws" — suspends until the response arrives; cancellation maps to CancellationError.

methods.rationale: A Swift async method returning the tuple (Data, URLResponse); since Swift surfaces failures as thrown errors rather than an NSError** out-param, the error pattern here is "exception" — i.e. `try await session.data(for: request)`.

patterns.note: Combine operator — emits the transform of the latest values from all upstreams.

methods.rationale: The trailing transform closure is *stored* for the publisher's entire lifetime — it's re-run on every upstream emission — and the operator returns a new, caller-owned publisher in the fluent Combine chain.

patterns.note: Touches the window server — promotes the window to key and brings it forward.

methods.rationale: Mutates on-screen UI state (key status + z-order), so like virtually all AppKit drawing/visibility calls it is "main-thread-only" — calling it off the main thread is undefined behavior; the `sender` argument is just borrowed.

patterns.note: Reads the filesystem; a shallow (non-recursive) directory listing.

methods.rationale: Returns an autoreleased-but-caller-owned NSArray* of URLs, or nil on failure with the trailing NSError** filled in — the standard "nil result -> consult *error" Foundation collection idiom.

patterns.note: KVC traversal — walks a dotted key path, e.g. "address.street".

methods.rationale: Key-Value Coding lookup: it can raise an NSException ("this class is not key value coding-compliant for the key ...") when the path is unknown — hence error_pattern "exception" rather than a returned error; the result is the usual autoreleased KVC value.

patterns.note: Tries to match a literal at the scan location and advances on success.

methods.rationale: Returns YES/NO for "did the literal match?"; the NSString** out-param (arg 1) is only meaningful when it returns YES — on NO it's left untouched, so callers must check the BOOL *before* trusting the captured substring.

patterns.note: Class-level convenience constructor in the sRGB-derived calibrated space.

methods.rationale: A class factory method — note `is_instance: false` — that vends an autoreleased NSColor from four 0.0–1.0 components; under ARC the "autoreleased" detail is invisible, but it's the correct ownership for the +colorWith… naming convention.

patterns.note: Drives the block once per match; set *stop = YES inside to bail early.

methods.rationale: The block is invoked *synchronously* once per match during the call and never stored — but there's a sharp re-entrancy caveat: "the block must not call back into the regex" (mutating or re-scanning mid-enumeration is unsupported).

patterns.note: Fire-and-forget dispatch — submits the work item and returns immediately.

methods.rationale: The work block is *escaping*: it's enqueued and run later on one of the queue's private worker threads — control returns to the caller before the block executes, which is the whole point of "async" (-> don't assume any ordering relative to the caller's next line).

patterns.note: Publishes NSPasteboardWriting items to the current pasteboard contents.

methods.rationale: Returns a BOOL but its key ownership fact is that the pasteboard *retains* the array's elements so they survive long after this call returns — "the clipboard owns a copy of your data until the next writer clears it."

patterns.note: Inserts a child into the view hierarchy; triggers layout/redraw.

methods.rationale: The parent *retains* the child for the duration of the parent-child relationship (a child has exactly one superview) and, because it mutates the live view tree, it is "main-thread-only" — off-main AppKit mutation is undefined.

patterns.note: Vends a suspended NSURLSessionDataTask — you must call -resume to start it.
Completion handler fires exactly once when the transfer ends.

methods.rationale: Returns a caller-owned (suspended) data task; the completion handler is *stored* until the transfer finishes, then "called once, on a background delegate queue" — so any UI work inside it must hop back to the main thread.

patterns.note: Writes the string to a POSIX path; "atomically:" uses a temp-file-then-rename dance.
Superseded by -writeToURL:atomically:encoding:error: — prefer URL-based APIs.

methods.rationale: Functionally the path-based twin of writeToURL:… (BOOL + trailing NSError**), but it's *deprecated* in favor of the URL variant — annotate it so callers get the "migrate to writeToURL:" nudge rather than silently using the legacy path API.

patterns.note: Formats a date into a localized string per the formatter's locale/format settings.

methods.rationale: Returns a caller-owned formatted string, but NSDateFormatter is *not* thread-safe — its internal CFDateFormatter state is mutated during formatting — so this is "owning-thread-only": callers sharing one formatter across threads must serialize access themselves.
